pm_mz:5.translation: Regarding 'specifically in private' — see the Taz: [The public recitation of the Ten Commandments] was originally read publicly as stated in tractate Tamid, but was abolished, as stated at the end of the first chapter of Berakhot, due to sectarian grievance — they would say the rest of the Torah is not true. But privately there is no such concern. See Berakhot 12a and Rashi's commentary. And the Drisha in the name of the Maharshals ruled that they may be said in public before 'Baruch She'amar,' for Rashi explained that they sought to establish [the Ten Commandments] as part of Shema — meaning with its blessings. So we rule according to the SA. However, each individual says them quietly in the synagogue, not aloud. And it is fitting to say them before 'Baruch She'amar,' as the Maharshals taught, for there are 72 words in Baruch She'amar and 'rosh ketem paz' [Shir HaShirim 5:11] equals 72, which is expounded on one speech, 'Anochi' — see there.
pm_mz:5.summary: The Mishbetzos Zahav explains the prohibition on publicly reciting the Ten Commandments (abolished by the Sages due to sectarians claiming only these commandments are obligatory) while affirming individual recitation. He records the Maharshals custom to say them before 'Baruch She'amar' and offers a kabbalistic reason (72 words of Baruch She'amar corresponding to the word 'paz' = 72), connected to the first commandment 'Anochi.'

pm_mz:6.translation: Regarding 'only by day' — see the Taz: The SA changed from the Tur's language, which wrote 'it is better [to say] only by day,' like the Tamid passage in MA siman 13. See the Tav-Shin commentary, letter yud-bet. And if one has no time during the day he may say them at night, and certainly [this applies to] Chatat and Asham.
pm_mz:6.summary: The Mishbetzos Zahav notes that the SA slightly altered the Tur's language regarding korbanot passages being restricted to daytime, and adds the practical ruling (citing an earlier authority) that if one has no opportunity during the day, the passages may be recited at night — with this leniency applying certainly to the Chatat and Asham passages.

pm_mz:7.translation: Regarding 'that come as nedavah' — see the Taz: The SA rules that after Asham one does not say [Yehi Ratzon], because Asham does not come as a nedavah — the halacha does not follow R. Eliezer in Keritot (25a) who held one may volunteer an Asham taluy, but follows the Rabbanan who hold Asham taluy cannot come as a nedavah. Asham Nazir — see the MA who cites [the ruling]. The R. [Akiva Eiger] 13 brings what the Maharshals ruled: one states it conditionally (mi-tenai), saying 'if I have sinned, may this be counted as if I brought a Chatat.' The Beit Chadash, in Eliyahu Rabbah 13, answered: state it as a condition saying 'let it be like Torah reading.' The Tav-Shin Beit commentary, letter tet, agreed.
pm_mz:7.summary: The Mishbetzos Zahav explains why the SA omits the 'Yehi Ratzon' after the Asham passage: since Asham cannot come as a voluntary offering (nedavah), one cannot meaningfully express the wish that one's recitation be equivalent to voluntarily bringing it. He records the Maharshals dissent — allowing a conditional formulation ('if I have sinned...') — and notes that others reframed it as equivalent to simply reading Torah rather than simulating an actual offering.

pm_mz:8.translation: Regarding the verse [ושחט] — see the Taz: R. [Akiva Eiger] 14, in the name of the Totza'ot Chayyim, says this verse should be said before the korbanot passages, and the korbanot passages should be said before 'Eizehu Mekoman.' This is correct: verse first (as Scripture), then explanation of Scripture, then Mishnah. The verse ושחט על ירך is from Vayikra 1:11. The Beit Yosef has 'ושחט לפני ה'' — a scribal error. We say [this verse] after the Tamid passage. It is fitting to say Tehillim before prayer, as they are 'Zemirot' to cut off accusers — [citing the] Levush and R. [Akiva Eiger] 14. The psukim dizimrah are Tehillim from 'Tehillah LeDavid' through 'Baruch ה' Le'olam' — so a Torah scholar should study before [prayer]. In the name of the Shelah: say Tehillim slowly, do not rush through verses — those who say and rush through while the listeners haven't finished, or those who answer and rush while the leader hasn't finished, are not reciting blessing but, God forbid, blasphemy. Similarly with Shirat HaYam — one recites a verse, then the next — rushing is not good. The Taz who wrote 'venashlimah farim sefateinu' [in place of the Tamid service] — it is a slip of the tongue, for the Tamid is actually a lamb — see the Tav-Shin commentary, letter dalet.
pm_mz:8.summary: The Mishbetzos Zahav establishes the correct order for the opening liturgical passages: the verse about slaughtering should precede the korbanot passages (which in turn precede the Mishnah 'Eizehu Mekoman'), maintaining the hierarchy of Scripture → explanation → Mishnah. He also cites the Shelah's strong criticism of those who rush through Tehillim and piyutim without waiting for the congregation, calling such rushing a desecration rather than a blessing.

er:1.translation: [Aleph] 'He shall strengthen himself like a lion etc.' The Acharonim wrote in the name of the Zohar: a person should not say 'I will lie in bed and study' — he must stand, dress himself, for [the verse commands] 'Prepare to meet your God, O Israel,' and if he can stand on his feet to avoid falling back asleep, that is very good. They also wrote that one should connect morning and evening, day and night, in Torah or prayer. The Reishit Chochmah, the Shelah, and other works of mussar elaborated at length on the great importance of rising at midnight and the punishment for one who neglects it — see there. They also wrote: one who rises at midnight to study should engage in Oral Torah. As for the timing of chatzot: the Sha'arei Tzion holds, in the name of the Zohar, that whether in summer (when nights are short) or winter (when nights are long), midnight is always 12 hours after the start of the night — exactly six actual hours after nightfall. This is logical, since the night always begins for everyone at the same starting point, as it is written 'And there was evening and there was morning.' In the Sefer Derekh Chochmah (page 46) the author seems to have understood that the night begins in summer several hours before actual nightfall — and therefore he challenged: how do we pray Mincha in summer within six hours of midnight? And why is work not forbidden on Friday afternoon in summer six hours before nightfall? He was not precise. It is also explained in the Zohar and Sha'arei Tzion that in winter, after 12 hours from the start of the night, even though it is still dark, [that hour] belongs to the day — both for chatzot and for the watches. As for the challenge: if so, we should permit Shacharit before dawn, and in summer not pray Shacharit until twelve hours after nightfall — it seems to me that the laws of prayer are different since they were established according to the times: Shacharit was established for the time one merits to see the day, as will be explained below. So we require actual daylight for Shacharit. There may be further hidden reasons. Nevertheless, it seems to me that the Bavli (Berakhot chapter 1) disagrees with the Zohar and holds that we count the night — both in summer and winter — in seasonal halachic hours, so chatzot is always exactly half the night. The Talmud's challenge — 'if we count the end of the last watch, why do we need a sign? It is already daytime!' — could only work on the Zohar's approach for the long nights of winter, but on the Babylonian approach it is always a question. Also the Gemara asks: the verse says 'At chatzot I rise' (Tehillim 119:62) — and also 'my eyes preceded the watches' (ibid. 148) — these two are consistent on the Zohar's approach too, since [on the Zohar's reading], the watches extend until chatzot and David rose before them... [continues with extensive analysis]. The Lechem Chamudot writes in Pirkei HaRo'eh: even though one must rise with alacrity and without laziness, nevertheless one should pause a moment — do not stand up immediately after sleep, for as it was taught: five things are close to death — one who eats and stands up, drinks and stands up, sleeps and stands up, lets blood and stands up, engages in marital relations and stands up. Rather, pause a moment and then rise.
er:1.summary: The Elya Rabbah opens with the Zohar's requirement to actually dress before studying Torah — one may not learn while remaining in bed. He then gives an extended analysis of the chatzot timing controversy between the Zohar (always 6 fixed hours after nightfall) and the Babylonian Talmud (seasonal hours, always exactly half the night). He defends the Zohar's position on its own terms while acknowledging the Talmud may disagree, and closes with the Lechem Chamudot's practical health advice not to stand up immediately upon waking.

er:2.translation: [Bet] 'In the morning etc.' 'Shomrim LaBboker' must be [said] before daylight even in summer. If it is close to sunrise one should say the kinot (lamentations) first and then begin 'Shomrim LaBboker,' skipping the kinot [in their usual place]. So writes R. Chayyim, and see Responsa Be'er Esek §124. The Knesset Gedolah (siman 619) writes: those people who rise early to synagogue every day and during prayer they doze — and I have seen even during the recitation of Shema that sleep overtakes them — about these I cite 'Woe to those who rise early in the morning.' Therefore, the right and proper course is: if they can also concentrate during prayer, they should rise early [for the watches]. But if on Shabbat they rise early and come [to synagogue] and then sleep during prayer, it is better for them to concentrate during prayer than to rise early — end quote. See later, siman 151 seif 2, that it is forbidden to sleep in the synagogue even a passing nap.
er:2.summary: The Elya Rabbah rules that Shomrim LaBboker must be recited before dawn even in summer, giving practical guidance about the order when sunrise is imminent. He then cites the Knesset Gedolah's important ruling that rising early for the watches is only praiseworthy if one still concentrates during the main prayer — if rising early causes dozing during Shema or Shemoneh Esreh, it is better to skip the early rising and daven properly.

er:3.translation: [Gimel] 'If a man hides etc.' — 'immediately fear will come upon him etc.' With this I explained [the phrase] at the start of the chapter 'Seder Ta'anit': the eldest among them would say words of rebuke (divrei kevushin) before them. Rashi explained: crushing their hearts to repentance. Tosafot, in the name of R. Chayyim, explained: that he reveals to them the secrets of their deeds, like 'among the hidden things of the Merciful One.' This is puzzling — is the elder a prophet? Rather it seems to me the meaning is: he tells them that the Holy One Blessed be He fills all the world with His glory and sees the hidden deeds — and this is the great rebuke that will bring fear upon them.
er:3.summary: The Elya Rabbah offers his own interpretation of the Gemara's phrase 'divrei kevushin' (words of rebuke) spoken by the elder on public fast days. Rejecting Tosafot's mysterious reading (the elder reveals hidden deeds as a prophet), he explains simply: the elder reminds the congregation that God sees all hidden things — thereby instilling genuine fear and bringing them to repentance.

er:4.translation: [Dalet] 'Upon awakening from sleep etc.' The Shelah (page 124) writes: if one does not wish to sleep, he should wash his hands even while remaining lying down. But it is forbidden to lie awake in bed without contemplating Torah. The Beit Chadash, siman 4, writes: in the Sefer Tola'at Yaakov it says in the name of the Zohar that one who walks four cubits in the morning without washing hands is liable to death. It is remarkable that the Beit Yosef did not write this. It seems that since the vast majority cannot be careful about this, it is better they sin unknowingly than knowingly — so he did not write it [end of Beit Chadash's language, which is difficult]. It also requires investigation: why does the world — even learned people — act leniently here? Furthermore, the Levush writes at the beginning of siman 25 'immediately upon arising from bed he should attend to his needs and wash his hands etc.' — and most people do not have a washroom within four cubits of their bed — implying one may be lenient. Also it seems from what the Levush wrote in siman 8, seif 11: 'if one wishes to don the tallit before washing in order not to walk four cubits without tzitzit etc.' — and this is easy to understand. My brother-in-law the Rabbi said that they rely on what the Shach writes in Yoreh Deah 344 in the name of the Mordechai — the entire room where the deceased lies counts as four cubits... [long discussion follows comparing the Mordechai's ruling for a corpse to the washing requirement, ultimately distinguishing the cases, with further analysis of the Talmud's two versions of the rule, Berakhot 25, and conclusion that fear-of-heaven types should be stringent and prepare a basin next to the bed].
er:4.summary: The Elya Rabbah addresses the Zohar's severe ruling that walking four cubits before morning handwashing is punishable by death, explaining why the Beit Yosef omitted it (most people cannot comply; better they sin unknowingly). He then extensively analyzes the halachic definition of 'four cubits' in this context — whether the whole room counts — drawing on the Beit Chadash, Levush, Shach, Mordechai, and Babylonian Talmud, concluding that G-d-fearing individuals should prepare water beside the bed to satisfy all opinions.

er:5.translation: [Heh] 'Few etc.' And similarly for Torah study: a person is asked 'Did you set fixed times for Torah?' — not 'How much Torah did you study?' For what good is it for a person to have learned twenty pereks in haste if five pereks in deliberateness and pleasantness is better. [Mekor Chayyim]
er:5.summary: The Elya Rabbah extends the principle of 'quality over quantity' from prayer to Torah study: God asks whether one set fixed, consistent times for learning — not how much one amassed. Slow, pleasant learning of five chapters surpasses rapid covering of twenty, paralleling the preference for few kavvanah-filled prayers over many distracted ones.

er:6.translation: [Vav] 'The Akeidah passage etc.' [The reason is] to remember the merit of the Patriarchs before God and to remember that one is obligated to sacrifice oneself for God. The Manna passage — so that one believes all his sustenance comes through Divine providence. The Sefer HaKavanot writes that [the Akeidah and Manna passages] should be said before the korbanot passages, and may be said even on Shabbat. The Shelah (page 202) writes that on Shabbat and Yom Tov one should say only the korbanot that were actually offered then: the Tamid, Musafin, and Ketoret — but not any of the other korbanot passages at all, even with the intention of merely reading Torah. [End of Shelah.] In the Eliyahu Zuta I cited the Lechem Chamudot (page 84): one who has the ability and desire to study Torah — it is better for him to study, and the set prayers are sufficient; in place of tehinnot and korbanot passages he should study — [end quote]. The abridged Shelah cited my words and challenged them, saying the Gemara holds 'the time of Torah is separate from the time of prayer.' He was not precise — for the Gemara refers to Shemoneh Esreh and Shema. Also he contradicts himself — in the laws of handwashing he ruled like my words. I also found this [position] in the Piskei Tosafot: one who is not busy with his affairs and is not a Torah scholar should not refrain from saying Ma'amadot and other prayers printed in the siddurim; but one who is busy with his affairs should refrain from them; and one who is a Torah scholar — it is better to study in their place... [continues with discussion of Tehillim recitation, whether the 'daily Psalm' must be said if not reciting Ma'amadot, customs about Shabbat Ma'amadot, and the Levush Orah distinguishing permissible requests from improper weeping on Shabbat].
er:6.summary: The Elya Rabbah explains the reasons behind the Akeidah (merit of Avot, self-sacrifice) and Manna (Divine providence) passages, and gives detailed practical rulings about Shabbat: one may say these passages on Shabbat, but should say only the korbanot actually offered on Shabbat (Tamid, Musaf, Ketoret). He records the important ruling of the Lechem Chamudot (echoing Tosafot) that Torah scholars should study instead of saying the additional piyyutim, and navigates a lengthy dispute about when learning may substitute for liturgical passages.

er:7.translation: [Zayin] 'Ten Commandments etc.' In the Eliyahu Zuta I wrote: the Maharshals was accustomed to say them before 'Baruch She'amar' because it has ten instances of 'Baruch' through 'Baruch Atah HaEl' [the Elya Rabbah notes that the Kitzur Shelah misidentified 'his grandfather' — it was the Maharshals' grandfather, not his own]. His grandfather (the Maharshals' grandfather) was accustomed to say the version from Parshat Yitro in the morning and the version from Parshat Va'etchanan in the evening after Shema al Mitato. The students of the Arizal write that he [the Ari] would say daily the four remembrances: Matan Torah, erasing Amalek, [the incident with] Miriam who became leprous — as it is written 'Remember what the Lord your God did to Miriam' — and also the Exodus from Egypt. The Shelah (page 128) writes: what is found written in our siddurim — the supplication arranged after the 72 verses, closed with the blessing 'Blessed are You O ה׳ who hears prayer' — and similarly some other tehinnot printed elsewhere are signed with a blessing — this is an error, for this blessing is not mentioned in the Talmud and Poskim, and one who recites it recites a blessing in vain. Rather say: 'Baruch Atah who hears prayer' — without mentioning the [Divine] Name. [End of Shelah.] See later in siman 46, seif 7, and the Taz at the end of siman 131.
er:7.summary: The Elya Rabbah records the Maharshals' and his grandfather's practices around the Ten Commandments: the Maharshals said them before Baruch She'amar, while his grandfather alternated versions by time of day (Yitro in the morning, Va'etchanan at night). He then records the Arizal's four daily remembrances, and closes with the Shelah's sharp ruling against ending personal prayers with a full Divine-Name blessing that is not established in the Talmud.

er:8.translation: [Chet] 'But in public it is forbidden etc.' — so they will not say that only this [section of] Torah is true. Therefore one does not write [the Ten Commandments] on a pamphlet designated for public [use]. [Lechem Chamudot]
er:8.summary: The Elya Rabbah briefly states the reason the Ten Commandments are prohibited in public prayer: the concern that people will conclude only the Ten Commandments are Divinely given, delegitimizing the rest of the Torah. Practically this extends to not printing them on public-use pamphlets, since communal recitation carries the same sectarian risk.

er:9.translation: [Tet] 'The korbanot passages etc.' Since it is written 'This is the Torah of the chatat (sin-offering)' — why [does the verse say] 'this is'? To teach that reading [the passage] is like offering it (Menachot 110). Rashi interprets differently: since it is not written 'chukat hachatat' (the statute of the chatat) — [the word 'Torah' rather than 'chok' teaches this equivalence]. The Levush challenged this — see there. In the Eliyahu Zuta I defended Rashi's words, arguing that he holds — as the Mizrachi writes in Parshat Chukat — that whenever only 'Torah' (not 'chukah') is written there is still a reason [accessible to human understanding]. And were one to challenge: but the Talmud in Menachot 19 asks about korbanot — 'wherever Torah and chukah are written it is indispensable, but korbanot have Torah written about them yet are not indispensable' — and it answers: Torah requires chukah, but chukah does not require Torah — therefore chukah was not written [about korbanot] so as not to imply they are all indispensable. Thus Rashi explains [that we derive the equivalence from] the word 'Torah' [precisely because 'chukah' was omitted]. In the Eliyahu Zuta I elaborated — see there. And my father and master (may he live) explained Rashi's words as: Rashi derives from the word 'Torah' — but asks: perhaps 'Torah' implies we should say they are all indispensable? He answers from the absence of 'chukah' — meaning, from 'Torah' alone we cannot derive indispensability; only from 'chukah' can we. Therefore only when 'Torah' appears [with 'chukah'] does it convey indispensability. And thus Rashi concludes his sharp analysis.
er:9.summary: The Elya Rabbah analyzes the Talmudic derivation (Menachot 110) that reciting the korbanot passages equals actually offering them, focusing on a dispute between the standard interpretation (from the word 'vezot') and Rashi's unique reading (from 'Torat' vs. 'Chukat'). He defends Rashi via the principle that 'Torah' implies a rationally accessible law while 'chukah' implies an indispensable statute, and incorporates a sharpening of this point from his own father.

taz:1.translation: He shall strengthen himself like a lion. The primary strength required is against the evil inclination, for [the Mishnah] says 'Who is strong etc.' (Avot 4:1). And the lion is specified because the lion's nature is to fear no creature, as it is written 'He is not dismayed by their cries etc.' (Yeshayahu 31:4) — likewise, no fear of the evil inclination should arise in a man, even though it is stronger than he.
taz:1.summary: The Taz zeroes in on the metaphor of the lion: the primary strength required of a Jew is not physical courage but mastery over the yetzer hara (evil inclination). The lion's fearlessness before any creature — even stronger ones — models how a person must refuse to be intimidated by the yetzer hara, even knowing it is more powerful than they are. This links the SA's opening word to the Mishnaic definition of a hero.

taz:2.translation: That he himself should awaken etc. At first glance this idea seems unrelated — it is simply about alacrity. The point is, however, that one cannot compare a person who performs a mitzvah through the inspiration of others to one who does so on his own. Based on this it seems we can explain what the Tur writes: 'I awaken the dawn, and the dawn does not awaken me' — the ending clause seems superfluous. Apparently, as we find: 'one who sanctifies himself below is sanctified above.' The same applies to inspiration — there are two kinds: first one is inspired 'from below' by himself, and afterwards higher inspiration comes to help. David praised himself for being on such a level — his own awakening from below was so great that he needed no heavenly inspiration, saying 'I awaken the dawn' — referring [in the Zohar's reading] to awakening the divine realm, a level belonging only to David and his like. But for us and those like us, we can only hope to achieve even the lower awakening, so that the higher one will come afterwards. Therefore the SA mentions only that one should at minimum 'awaken the dawn,' even without meriting the second higher level.
taz:2.summary: The Taz explains why the SA includes the phrase 'he himself shall awaken the dawn': it is not a separate instruction about timing but about the quality of religious inspiration. Two levels exist — self-generated (from below) and divinely granted (from above); David needed only the first. The SA sets a modest goal for ordinary people: achieve at minimum the self-generated awakening, so that divine assistance may follow.

taz:3.translation: A little etc. The Tur's language reads: 'One who increases and one who decreases — provided he directs his heart in his supplications, for few with kavvanah are better than many without.' This is difficult: if the Tur first means one who increases with kavvanah, why compare him to the one who decreases as equal? And if without kavvanah, why say afterwards 'for it is better etc.' — contradicting the equation made earlier? The Gemara at the end of Menachot says: 'One who increases and one who decreases are equal, provided he directs his heart to Heaven.' It seems from the story in Shabbat 10a — where Rava saw R. Hamnuna prolonging his prayer and said 'They neglect eternal life and occupy themselves with momentary life' — that Rava held Torah-time and prayer-time are separate. Thus one who increases in prayer [like R. Hamnuna] holds they are separate [and thus is not neglecting Torah]; and one who decreases [like Rava] wishes to occupy himself with Torah. Therefore the Tur concludes well: 'provided he directs his heart to Heaven' — meaning: the reason he decreases his prayer should be for Heaven's sake, so he can concentrate. On this the Tur says: sometimes increasing prayer is the cause of losing kavvanah, so it is better to shorten and concentrate. Hence: one who increases [for proper reasons] and one who decreases [to concentrate] are both valid, but the reason for decreasing must be for Heaven's sake — that is the lesson.
taz:3.summary: The Taz resolves an internal contradiction in the Tur's formulation about prayer quantity: the clause 'provided he directs his heart' qualifies the one who decreases — his decrease must be motivated by the desire to concentrate better, not laziness. He supports this from the Shabbat 10a story contrasting R. Hamnuna (who prolonged prayer) with Rava (who shortened to devote time to Torah), showing that both approaches are valid when motivated by Heaven.

taz:4.translation: The Akeidah passage. To remember the merit of the Patriarchs each day, and also to subdue one's evil inclination — as Yitzchak surrendered his life [in the Akeidah]. The Manna passage — so that one believes all his sustenance comes through Providence. The korbanot passages — as the Gemara at the end of Menachot says: 'This is the Torah of the Chatat — all who engage with the Torah of the chatat it is as if they offered a chatat etc.'
taz:4.summary: The Taz provides the rationale for three of the daily passages: the Akeidah (evoking Patriarchal merit and modeling self-surrender to God), the Manna section (inculcating trust in Divine providence over sustenance), and the korbanot passages (their recitation equivalently replaces actual Temple offerings, derived from Menachot 110). He is notably concise compared to other commentators on this seif.

taz:5.translation: Specifically in private etc. [The Ten Commandments] were originally read publicly as stated in tractate Tamid; and as stated at the end of the first chapter of Berakhot, they were abolished due to sectarian grievance — they would say only that part of the Torah is true. This concern applies to public reading, but not to private recitation.
taz:5.summary: The Taz briefly explains the distinction between public and private recitation of the Ten Commandments: communal recitation was abolished by the Sages because sectarians used it to claim the rest of the Torah is not divine. Private recitation carries no such communal implication, hence an individual may say them daily.

taz:6.translation: Only by day. Since their [the sacrifices'] time is only by day.
taz:6.summary: The Taz provides the simple reason for limiting the korbanot passages to daytime: the actual Temple sacrifices were offered exclusively during daylight hours, and the recitation parallels the offering.

taz:7.translation: That come as nedavah (voluntary offerings). Unlike the Chatat, which cannot be brought unless one has transgressed a sin requiring a chatat, and the Asham — even though we find an asham taluy (guilt offering for doubt) and one may bring oneself into nazirite status and thereby bring an asham — nonetheless a standard asham does not come as a nedavah. I have seen in the name of the Maharshals that one can say the Yehi Ratzon also after the chatat and asham, only using doubtful language — 'May it be Your will that if I have committed a sin requiring a chatat, this should be considered as if I offered a chatat.' In my view this is incorrect: certainly we find in the Gemara in many places that one may bring an Olah conditionally — 'if I am obligated for an Olah let this be an Olah, and if not let it be a voluntary Olah' — as in the passage about a found animal. But we never find such a conditional for a chatat, since a chatat cannot come as a nedavah — how can one say 'if I am not obligated, let this be a voluntary chatat'? There is no such thing as a voluntary chatat-offering. Thus: if one is indeed obligated a chatat and the reading counts as that chatat, how can one add 'and if not, let it be a nedavah,' when the reading has already been performed in the manner of a chatat with all its particulars? Therefore one should not say the Yehi Ratzon [after chatat/asham]. I have also seen a responsum [rebutting those] who say 'venashlimah farim sefateinu' before the Tamid passage and Mishnayot of Eizehu Mekoman — in my view this is incorrect and should be protested. [This phrase implies] everything said afterwards is included, and [the passage of] chatat laws is mentioned there, so saying 'venashlimah farim' as a voluntary offering before that is problematic — it is as if one is bringing unconsecrated [animals] into the Temple courtyard. Unless one says 'venashlimah farim' specifically in place of the Tamid service [i.e., after the Tamid passage] as I wrote above.
taz:7.summary: The Taz rules against the Maharshals' position that allows conditional 'Yehi Ratzon' formulas after the Chatat and Asham passages. His logic: a Chatat can never come as a voluntary offering (nedavah), so one cannot conditionally say 'if I am not obligated, let this be a voluntary Chatat' — no such category exists. He also rules against saying 'venashlimah farim sefateinu' before the Tamid passage, as this would implicitly include the Chatat section under a voluntary-offering formula, which is halachically untenable.

taz:8.translation: The passage 'ושחט' etc. As the Midrash states: God declared, 'I testify upon Myself — whether gentile or Israelite — at the moment they read this verse [about slaughtering on the north side of the altar], I remember the Akeidah of Yitzchak.'
taz:8.summary: The Taz explains the significance of reciting the verse 'And he shall slaughter it on the north side of the altar before ה'' — God Himself declared that whenever this verse is read, regardless of who reads it, He recalls the Akeidah of Yitzchak. This gives the verse special intercessory power and explains why the SA singles it out for daily recitation alongside the korbanot passages.

ma:1.translation: That he himself should awaken the dawn. The Shelah writes there is a mystical idea to connect day and night in Torah or in prayer — whether in the morning or in the evening.
ma:1.summary: The Magen Avraham cites the Shelah's kabbalistic teaching that one should strive to be engaged in Torah or prayer at the moment the day transitions to night and vice versa — linking both liminal moments in divine service. This 'connecting of day and night' is presented as a spiritual secret (sod) rather than a strict halachic obligation.

ma:2.translation: Before them. Nevertheless one should not quarrel with them (Beit Yosef).
ma:2.summary: The Magen Avraham adds a critical qualification to the Rema's instruction to be bold before mockers: one must not descend to quarreling or fighting with them. The Beit Yosef grounds this in the principle that the virtue of firmness in mitzvot must not become the vice of combativeness.

ma:3.translation: Immediately. This is not precise — rather he should wait a moment, as stated in Gittin 70.
ma:3.summary: The Magen Avraham corrects the SA's phrase 'immediately he should arise with alacrity' — the Talmud (Gittin 70) lists sudden rising from sleep among five behaviors dangerous to health. One should pause briefly before standing, making the halachic instruction medically responsible.

ma:4.translation: One who rises early. The Kabbalists elaborated greatly on the significance of midnight prayer. The Recanati in Parashat Bereishit writes: 'Some say the time for seeking mercy is the end of the night. I reconcile [this]: at chatzot it is fitting to seek mercy for the congregation of Israel and its children etc.; and at the end of the night one should seek his personal needs, and this prayer requires nefilat apayim.' See siman 131 seif 2. From our Talmud: according to the first answer [in Berakhot 3a], which gives a sign for the middle of the middle watch — namely chatzot — it is implied that this is a time of divine favor. According to the second answer, this is not implied. See the Zohar Vayakhel p. 342: the night is always counted as 12 [fixed] hours, in summer as in winter, even though for prayer it is not so — see siman 233. The Sha'arei Tzion also writes this.
ma:4.summary: The Magen Avraham documents the kabbalistic significance of midnight prayer, citing the Recanati's division of the night into three prayer opportunities: chatzot for communal intercession, end of night for personal needs (with nefilat apayim). He notes the Talmudic debate (Berakhot 3a) over whether chatzot is intrinsically auspicious, and cites the Zohar's ruling that midnight is always exactly 6 fixed hours after nightfall regardless of season — unlike the seasonal hours used for prayer times.

ma:5.translation: The destruction of the Beit HaMikdash. The Shelah writes that at every meal one should say 'Al Naharot Bavel' and on Shabbat one should say 'Mizmor Beshuv ה' et Shivat Tzion' etc., and after setting one's heart [in proper intention] those are the very words [that fulfill the obligation].
ma:5.summary: The Magen Avraham extends the obligation to mourn the Churban beyond prayer into daily meals: the Shelah prescribes 'Al Naharot Bavel' (Tehillim 137) at each weekday meal and 'Mizmor Beshuv' (Tehillim 126) at Shabbat meals, so that even eating is accompanied by ongoing consciousness of the Temple's absence.

ma:6.translation: A little. The Gemara says: 'One who increases and one who decreases — provided he directs his heart to Heaven' — meaning that even though the one who increases also has kavvanah, nevertheless, if the one who decreases was unable [due to circumstances] to say more, he is as pleasing to God as the one who increases. This is implied by the Gemara's proof — the burnt offering of a poor person also has the phrase 'a pleasing aroma' just like the burnt offering of the wealthy person.
ma:6.summary: The Magen Avraham clarifies the Talmudic principle from Menachot: both the person who says many prayers and the one who says few are equally valued by God — provided the one who says fewer does so due to inability to say more, not laziness. The proof is the bird offering of the poor person, which receives the same 'pleasing aroma' designation as the more valuable animal offering, affirming that circumstantial limitation does not diminish the worshipper's standing.

ma:7.translation: The Akeidah. The Bachya in Parshat Tzav writes that it is not sufficient to merely recite [the passage] — one must explain the parasha [to oneself] and recognize God's wonders. And similarly he explains the saying 'Whoever recites Tehillah LeDavid three times daily is assured a place in the World to Come.'
ma:7.summary: The Magen Avraham, citing Bachya ibn Paquda, insists that mere verbal recitation of the Akeidah is insufficient — one must understand the text and internalize recognition of God's wonders. This principle extends to Tehillah LeDavid (Ashrei): its three-times-daily recitation earns a share in the World to Come only through genuine comprehension, not mechanical repetition.

ma:8.translation: Olah. After the Olah, Shelamim, and Todah [passages], one should say the nesachim (libation) passage, found in Parshat Shelach Lecha — for there is no animal offering without nesachim. But Chatat and Asham do not require nesachim. The passage 'Tzav et Aharon' is not relevant to the Olah — it pertains to the Tamid offering. And one should say the passage 'Veshama kol Eleh' at the end of Vayikra, which concerns oaths of utterance, oaths of testimony, a certain asham, and an asham taluy. I was asked: why do the Tur and Beit Yosef in the SA order Olah before Chatat — yet we rule in Pirkei Zevachim [10] that Chatat precedes Olah? And Rashi in Parshat Tazria also writes this — see the Ra'em and the following. In my view: the Gemara in Zevachim 7 says [the reason Chatat precedes Olah] — 'This is like an advocate who enters [before the king] to appease [before] the gift [enters].' And Tosafot write that even though the Olah atones for positive commandments more than the Chatat — nevertheless, once the Chatat is offered first, it has already atoned for [sins requiring] karet and positive commandments, and so the Olah then comes as a gift. But in our recitation the Tur rules not to say Yehi Ratzon after the Chatat passage — for perhaps one has not sinned and it would be like reading Torah only. But the Olah atones for positive commandments — and the Gemara says there is no person not obligated for a positive commandment — so one should say Olah first, since it always applies. But if one knows he has incurred a Chatat obligation, he should say the Chatat passage first.
ma:8.summary: The Magen Avraham provides crucial practical guidance: after the Olah, Shelamim, and Todah passages one should add the nesachim section (since all these offerings require libations), but not after Chatat and Asham (which do not). He resolves the apparent reversal of Temple order (where Chatat precedes Olah) by explaining that in recitation the Olah comes first because everyone is perpetually obligated for positive commandments (hence Olah always applies), whereas the Chatat 'Yehi Ratzon' is only said when one knows he sinned.

ma:9.translation: In public. Because of the sectarians (minim) who say 'there is no Torah except this' — and especially in our times. Therefore one does not write [the Ten Commandments] on a pamphlet designated for the public. (Lekhem Chamudot §9)
ma:9.summary: The Magen Avraham reinforces the prohibition on public recitation of the Ten Commandments, noting that the sectarian concern applies with even greater force 'in our times' (possibly referring to Christian or heretical groups emphasizing the Decalogue). Practically this extends to not publishing them in communal booklets, as communal written distribution carries the same risk as communal oral recitation.

bh:1.translation: Like a lion. See the Halakhot Ketanot vol. 2 §3 and the Sefer Bnei Chayya.
bh:1.summary: The Be'er Hetev briefly directs the reader to two additional sources that expand on the opening instruction to strengthen oneself like a lion: the Halakhot Ketanot (responsa of R. Yaakov Chaggiz) and the Sefer Bnei Chayya, both of which contain further halachic and moral elaboration on the theme of morning alacrity.

bh:2.translation: The dawn. The Shelah writes there is a mystical idea to connect day and night through Torah or prayer, whether morning or evening. And immediately upon waking — if one does not wish to sleep more — he should wash his hands, even while still lying down. And through this he will be protected from nocturnal impurity (keri). And certainly [this applies] if he is not planning to sleep — Shelah. The Sefer Tola'at Yaakov writes: it is stated in the Zohar that one who walks four cubits in the morning without washing hands is liable to death. The Beit Chadash was puzzled why R. [Yosef Karo] the Beit Yosef did not write this. It appears [the reason is] that the majority cannot be careful about this, and it is better they sin unknowingly than that they sin knowingly [in willful defiance]. The Shiurei Knesset HaGedolah and the Damascus Eli'ezer write this. See also the Yad Aharon siman 4 in the Hagahot HaTur.
bh:2.summary: The Be'er Hetev collects several rulings about handwashing upon waking: the Shelah's teaching about connecting day and night through Torah/prayer, the important ruling to wash hands even while still lying down (to avoid nocturnal impurity), and the Zohar's severe warning about walking four cubits before washing. He explains why the Beit Yosef omitted the Zohar's ruling — most people cannot comply, so better they sin unknowingly — citing multiple later authorities who confirm this reasoning.

bh:3.translation: I have set ה' [before me always]. One should visualize the [four-letter Divine] Name constantly before his eyes, written thus: יִהְוָה — and this is the secret of 'I have set ה' before me always.' This is of great benefit for yirat shamayim (fear of Heaven). [Arizal]
bh:3.summary: The Be'er Hetev transmits the Arizal's mystical practice for implementing the Rema's principle of Sheviti Hashem: one should literally visualize the Divine Name written with the vowelization of the word 'yir'ah' (fear) — יִהְוָה — perpetually before the mind's eye. This kabbalistic technique operationalizes the abstract idea of constant divine awareness into a concrete contemplative practice.

bh:4.translation: Before mockers. Nevertheless do not quarrel with them. Beit Yosef.
bh:4.summary: The Be'er Hetev, citing the Beit Yosef, qualifies the instruction to be bold before mockers: boldness must stop at quarreling. One maintains inner confidence in mitzvot without descending to interpersonal conflict, since combativeness is itself a character flaw that must not be cultivated even in the service of heaven.

bh:5.translation: With alacrity. This is not precise — rather he should wait a bit and not stand up suddenly (Gittin 70). The Seder HaYom writes: upon rising one should immediately say 'Modeh Ani lifanecha Melech chai vekayam shehechazarta bi nishmati bechemlah, rabah emunatecha.' There is no need for handwashing for this — even if the hands are unclean — since there is no mention of [the Divine] Name or epithet in this text. Nevertheless, to study [Torah] before handwashing appears to be forbidden. The Eliyahu Zuta. And after handwashing, one should say the 42-letter Name (Ana Bekoach).
bh:5.summary: The Be'er Hetev corrects the SA's 'immediately' with the Talmudic health ruling (Gittin 70) to pause briefly before rising. He then records the Seder HaYom's formula of Modeh Ani — which may be said before handwashing since it contains no Divine Name — and adds the Eliyahu Zuta's ruling that Torah study before handwashing is forbidden even if Modeh Ani is permitted, rounding off with the kabbalistic custom to recite Ana Bekoach after washing.

bh:6.translation: And at the end of the night. This is the time of 'Shomrim LaBboker,' [and it must be before dawn] even in summer. And if one is close to sunrise one should first say the lament (kinah) and then begin 'Shomrim LaBboker.' Derekh Chochmah. Regarding midnight prayer, see the Sefer HaKavanot and the Zohar Vayakhel — [from there] it seems the night is always counted as 12 hours, in summer or in winter, even though for prayer it is not so — see siman 233, Magen Avraham. See also the Eliyahu Rabbah [above]. The later authorities have an accepted tradition: from 'Shichvi' to 'Kumi' — meaning from the Shavuot period when they say 'Shichvi' to Tisha B'Av when they say 'Kumi' — one need not rise at night. Some say from the 15th of Av to the 15th of Iyar one should rise. The appropriate amount [of sleep] is 8 hours, and the sign is: 'I slept, and then nothing disturbs me' (Iyov 3:13). Everything depends on the individual. The Arizal writes that learning Mishnayot should come first before everything else [after chatzot] — and through this one merits the neshamah (soul), for 'Mishnah' has the same letters as 'neshamah.' If one has merited the true wisdom [kabbalah], that time is extremely auspicious.
bh:6.summary: The Be'er Hetev addresses the timing and customs of the midnight watch: Shomrim LaBboker must precede dawn even in summer, with instructions for when sunrise is imminent. He summarizes the chatzot timing debate (fixed vs. seasonal hours), records the accepted custom about when midnight rising is not required (Shavuot to Tisha B'Av), notes varying customs for the rest of the year, gives the Arizal's instruction to learn Mishnayot first (with a gematria linking Mishnah/Neshamah), and affirms that kabbalists find this time especially spiritually potent.

bh:7.translation: That he should grieve. But Torah study and prayer should be done with joy. Solet Belulah.
bh:7.summary: The Be'er Hetev adds a crucial balance: while the seif requires grief over the Churban as an ongoing obligation, Torah and prayer must be performed with simchah (joy). The grief is specific to the mourning-focused aspect of one's service, not a general mood that pervades all religious activity — a distinction the Solet Belulah explicitly draws.

bh:8.translation: With kavvanah. Meaning: one who is compelled by circumstances and cannot prolong [his prayer] and instead says few supplications with kavvanah — he is considered before the Holy One Blessed be He the same as one who has leisure and prolongs his supplications with kavvanah. [Mishnah Berurah, Yad Aharon.] See also the Taz and the responsa Shevut Yaakov vol. 2 §44. And see the Tosafot Yom Tov in tractate Avot chapter 2, Mishnah 16. The Sefer Mekor Chayyim writes that the same principle applies to Torah study. And so writes the Solet Belulah.
bh:8.summary: The Be'er Hetev clarifies the ruling's scope: the preference for few supplications with kavvanah over many without applies specifically to one who is forced by circumstances to pray briefly. Such a person is valued equally before God as one who has time and can pray extensively with kavvanah. He extends this equalization principle to Torah study (citing Mekor Chayyim), reinforcing that sincere effort within one's capacity is what God measures.

bh:9.translation: The Akeidah passage [is said] before the korbanot passages, and one may say the Akeidah and Manna passages even on Shabbat. The Eliyahu Rabbah and so writes the author of Shnei Yadot and the Solet Belulah and the Knesset HaGedolah — see there. The Bachya in Parshat Tzav writes that it is not sufficient to merely recite [the Akeidah] — one must understand the parasha and recognize God's wonders. And so he explains the saying 'Whoever recites Tehillah LeDavid three times a day is assured a place in the World to Come.'
bh:9.summary: The Be'er Hetev rules that the Akeidah passage should precede the korbanot passages in the liturgical order, and that both the Akeidah and Manna passages may be said on Shabbat (unlike certain other korbanot passages). He then cites Bachya's standard: recitation is not enough — understanding God's wonders through the text is required — and applies this equally to the three-daily recitation of Ashrei.

mb:1.summary: The Mishnah Berurah explains the foundational motivation for morning alacrity: man was created for divine service ('all who are called by My name, I created for My glory'). He then provides a powerful practical counter to the yetzer hara's seasonal arguments (too cold in winter, too tired in summer): imagine how carefully one would arise to serve a human king — how much more so before the King of kings.

mb:2.summary: The Mishnah Berurah transmits the Shelah's kabbalistic teaching about connecting day and night, then gives detailed practical halachah about morning handwashing: wash immediately on waking (even while still lying down), do not walk four cubits without washing. The Zohar is severe about the punishment for this. However, one may not violate other prohibitions in order to wash (e.g., grabbing another's water, or suppressing urination), and practical guidance is given for when water is far. Torah study before handwashing is forbidden regardless.

mb:3.summary: The Mishnah Berurah explains the Rema's addition: even when there is still time for prayer before the deadline, praying with the congregation is itself a mitzvah. He cross-references seif katan 9 below, where this point is developed further.

mb:4.summary: The Mishnah Berurah operationalizes 'Sheviti ה'' as a meditation: constantly envision God's presence, and specifically (citing the Arizal) visualize the Divine Name written with the vowels of 'yir'ah' — יִהְוָה. He then addresses the practical custom of Shiviti parchments in siddurim or synagogues, ruling that parchments behind glass (in shuls) are permissible but unprotected parchments near candles must be protested, due to the danger of erasing the Divine Name.

mb:5.summary: The Mishnah Berurah, quoting the Beit Yosef at length, rules that even while standing firm against mockers, one must never quarrel with them. The reason is profound: brazenness is an extremely base character trait, and using it even for mitzvot causes it to become embedded in one's personality — so that one becomes brazen even in contexts where divine service is not at stake.

mb:6.summary: The Mishnah Berurah addresses the average person among great scholars: do not be embarrassed to perform mitzvot. However, if one can perform them privately, that is even better. But in the presence of ordinary people who might learn from example, performing the mitzvah publicly is preferable — as long as the intention is purely for Heaven's sake, not for self-glorification.

mb:7.summary: The Mishnah Berurah clarifies the Rema's phrase 'also in modesty/privacy': even when alone at home, one's conduct must reflect the same awareness of standing before God as in public. This extends the principle of Sheviti ה' into every private moment of one's day.

mb:8.summary: The Mishnah Berurah corrects 'immediately' with the Gittin 70 health ruling (do not stand up suddenly). He then introduces Modeh Ani as the first utterance upon waking — it requires no handwashing since it contains no Divine Name, though Torah study remains forbidden until hands are washed, and Birkhot HaTorah are required before any Torah learning.

mb:9.summary: The Mishnah Berurah provides comprehensive guidance on Tikkun Chatzot: midnight is always exactly 12 hours after noon regardless of season; one mourns the Churban just before midnight, studies Torah from midnight, and makes personal requests toward dawn. He records the accepted tradition exempting Tikkun Chatzot from Shavuot to Tisha B'Av, gives instructions for public chatzot gatherings (best in the shul itself), strongly warns against idle talk during night waking, and rules that those constitutionally unable to wake early without dozing during prayer should instead sleep fully but wake at least a half-hour before the morning call to shul.
